title: Brazilian Carrot Cake with Chocolate Glaze
procedure: In a blender, combine the grated carrots, eggs, and vegetable oil until smooth.
Add the sugar and blend again until fully incorporated.
In a large bowl, add the all-purpose flour and mix well with the blended liquid mixture.
Gently fold in the baking powder until just combined.
Pour the batter into a greased and floured baking pan and bake in a preheated oven at 180°C for approximately 40 minutes.
For the glaze, mix the butter, cocoa powder, sugar, and milk in a saucepan.
Cook over low-to-medium heat, stirring constantly until it thickens, then pour immediately over the baked cake.
note: A timeless comfort food classic. The secret is to mix the baking powder very gently at the end so the batter rises perfectly fluffy.

title: Fluffy Chocolate Cake with Brigadeiro
components: 2 cans sweetened condensed milk (395g each - brigadeiro)
4 tbsp cocoa powder 50% or chocolate powder (brigadeiro)
1 can heavy cream / table cream (200g - brigadeiro)
procedure: In a bowl, whisk the eggs and sugar together. Slowly add the oil, mixing well.
Add the 50% cocoa powder and stir until smooth.
Pour in the warm water (or milk) and a pinch of salt, mixing gently.
Sift the flour into the batter gradually, incorporating it calmly and patiently.
Add the baking powder and mix gently just until combined. Pour into a greased and floured pan (size 20x10cm recommended) and bake at 180°C for 40 to 50 minutes until a toothpick comes out clean.
For the brigadeiro, mix the condensed milk and cocoa powder in a saucepan before turning on the heat. Add the butter and heavy cream.
Cook over medium heat, stirring constantly, until it reaches the thick 'soft ribbon' consistency. Transfer to a bowl, cover with plastic wrap touching the surface, and let cool.

Once both the cake and brigadeiro are at room temperature, slice the cake into two layers. Spread a portion of the brigadeiro on the bottom layer, place the top layer back on, poke light holes with a fork (optional for syrup), and cover the entire cake with the remaining brigadeiro. Decorate with sprinkles.
note: Made entirely by hand without a mixer, resulting in an incredibly fluffy texture that pairs perfectly with the rich thickness of the brigadeiro.

title: Milk & White Chocolate Ice Cream
procedure: In a saucepan, whisk together the egg yolks, milk, and sugar.
Add the milk powder and condensed milk, stirring until completely dissolved.
Place over heat and drop in the white chocolate, stirring continuously until melted and smooth.
Remove from heat, let it cool to room temperature, and then chill in the refrigerator for 3 hours.
In a separate bowl, whip the heavy cream until it forms stiff peaks.
Gently fold the whipped cream into the chilled milk base until uniform.
Pour the mixture into popsicle molds or a container, and freeze for at least 10 hours.
note: A rich, velvety base. Patience during the freezing process is essential to achieve the perfect, crystallization-free texture.

title: Grandma's Greek Kayanas (Strapatsada)
components: 10 ripe tomatoes (peeled, solid flesh only, seeds/gel removed)
Toasted bread and extra virgin olive oil for serving
procedure: Chop the peeled and seeded solid tomato flesh into pieces.
Heat a pan containing 3-4 tablespoons of olive oil until hot.
Add the tomatoes to the hot pan and cook, stirring occasionally with a wooden spoon.
Simmer for about 20 minutes until the excess tomato juice evaporates and the mixture is nearly dry (a line drawn across the pan with the spoon should stay clear).
In a bowl, whisk the 8 eggs with half a teaspoon of salt, then pour them directly into the pan with the tomatoes.
Stir constantly and intensely for 5-10 minutes. Halfway through, incorporate the paprika, cayenne pepper, and black pepper.
Once the mixture thickens and loses its liquid consistency, remove from heat.
Serve hot on top of toasted bread with a light, fresh drizzle of olive oil.
note: A traditional Greek recipe shared by MikeHan. While many in Greece use more eggs than tomatoes, this specific version honors his grandmother's family ratio, prioritizing a rich, concentrated tomato base over the eggs.

title: Traditional Brazilian Brigadeiro
components: A small amount of butter for greasing your hands
procedure: In a saucepan, combine the sweetened condensed milk, cocoa powder, and unsalted butter before turning on the heat.
Place the saucepan over low-to-medium heat and stir continuously with a spatula, making sure to scrape the bottom and sides of the pan.
Cook for about 10 to 15 minutes, or until the mixture thickens and easily slides away from the bottom of the pan when tilted (the 'slide' point).
Pour the mixture onto a greased plate or shallow dish and let it cool completely to room temperature.
Once cooled, grease your hands with a little bit of butter, scoop small portions with a spoon, and roll them into neat little balls.
Roll each ball in chocolate sprinkles until fully coated, then place them into paper candy cups.
note: The ultimate Brazilian party icon. For the perfect texture, avoid high heat so the sugar doesn't crystallize, ensuring a smooth, chewy bite every time.

title: Smoked Sausage and Onion Farofa
components: 2 cups cassava flour (farinha de mandioca, toasted or raw)
1 large onion, thinly sliced or chopped
Salt and black pepper to taste
Finely chopped fresh parsley and scallions for garnish
procedure: Heat the olive oil in a large skillet or pan over medium heat and add the diced smoked sausage.
Sauté the sausage until it releases its own flavorful fat and turns nicely golden and crispy.
Add the butter to the pan, and once melted, toss in the chopped onion and minced garlic.
Sauté the mixture until the onions become soft, translucent, and slightly caramelized at the edges.
Gradually add the cassava flour while stirring constantly to evenly absorb the fats and flavors.
Keep stirring over low heat for about 5 minutes until the flour is beautifully toasted and golden.
Season with salt and black pepper to taste, remove from heat, and mix in the fresh parsley and scallions before serving.
note: The perfect, crunchy companion for a traditional barbecue or feijoada. If the farofa feels a bit too dry for your liking, simply melt in an extra tablespoon of butter at the very end.
